text: The surgeon observes the dilated proximal bowel and prepares to decompress it using a two-oh silk suture to create a purse string in the area planned for resection.
The surgeon ties off the colotomy and proceeds to suspend the uterus in preparation for the bowel resection.
The surgeon observes the adherence of the colon to the left adnexa and begins dissection from medial to lateral, starting underneath the superior hemorrhoidal artery over the sacral promontory.
The surgeon identifies the left colic artery branching from the superior hemorrhoidal artery, planning to preserve it while performing lateral mobilization of the colon.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the superior hemorrhoidal artery, performing medial dissection before proceeding to lateral dissection.

The surgeon addresses the adherence of the colon to the left adnexa, noting the large and redundant colon which negates the need for extensive lateral dissection or flexure mobilization. Urethral stents are used to aid in the dissection process, particularly in cases with diverticular strictures that are scarred against the left sidewall.
The surgeon completes the dissection and resection, finalizing the separation of the colon from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon performs a standard sigmoid resection, examining the planned distal margin and bowel caliber. Further rectal dissection is conducted to facilitate reaching the anastomosis and transecting the rectal mesentery. The proximal mesentery is then addressed.
The surgeon checks the ICG fluorescence to assess the vascular supply of the bowel, highlighting its chronic dilation. Two 60mm staplers are used to transect the dilated bowel segment.

The surgeon performs a sewn anastomosis on the rectal side, using a two-layer technique. The outer row is sewn with 2-0 PDS sutures, starting at the confluence of the two staple lines on the proximal colon side. The proximal colon is offset to the patient's left to ensure proper alignment for the end-to-end anastomosis.

The surgeon creates collatomies, ensuring similar sizes on both ends, and checks the ICG fluorescence to assess the perfusion of the inner lip of the anastomosis. The inner row is sewn using a three-oh V-lock suture, with the outer row intact behind it.

The surgeon uses a two-O PDS suture to complete the outer anterior row of the anastomosis, tying it to the posterior PDS to ensure a complete two-layer anastomosis. The surgical field is cleaned, and the surgeon checks the anastomosis from the posterior aspect for any need to over-sew. A leak test is performed, showing a widely patent anastomosis with no bleeding or bubbles. The specimen is prepared for extraction, and the uterus is repositioned.
